beginner.title: Beginner DSA Roadmap
beginner.description: Start your DSA journey from scratch! This roadmap covers all fundamental concepts you need to crack coding interviews at top tech companies. Follow the steps in order for best results.

steps.title: Programming Fundamentals
steps.desc: Master basic programming constructs, variables, loops, conditionals, and functions.
steps.theory:
Getting Started with Programming

Before diving into DSA, ensure you have a solid grasp of basic programming concepts including variables, data types, loops (for, while), conditionals (if-else), functions, and basic I/O operations.

Key Concepts

  • Variables and Data Types
  • Control Flow (if/else, switch)
  • Loops (for, while, do-while)
  • Functions and Scope
  • Basic Input/Output
  • Debugging Techniques

steps.title: Arrays & Strings
steps.desc: Learn array manipulation, string operations, and fundamental patterns like two-pointer and sliding window.
steps.theory:
Arrays

Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, giving O(1) random access. Master common operations: traversal, insertion, deletion, and searching.

Strings

Strings are sequences of characters. Learn pattern matching, palindrome checking, anagram detection, and substring problems.

Key Patterns

  • Two Pointers
  • Sliding Window
  • Prefix Sum
  • Kadane's Algorithm

steps.title: Recursion Basics
steps.desc: Understand recursive thinking, base cases, recursion trees, and problem decomposition.
steps.theory:
Understanding Recursion

Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller subproblems. Every recursive solution has two parts: a base case and a recursive case.

Key Concepts

  • Base Case and Recursive Case
  • Call Stack and Stack Overflow
  • Recursion Trees
  • Tail Recursion
  • Divide and Conquer Strategy

steps.title: Searching Algorithms
steps.desc: Master linear search, binary search, and their variations for sorted data.
steps.theory:
Searching Fundamentals

Searching is the process of finding a specific element in a collection. Learn both linear and binary search with their complexity trade-offs.

Binary Search

One of the most important algorithms. Works on sorted arrays by repeatedly dividing the search space in half. O(log n) time complexity.

Variations

  • First/Last Occurrence
  • Search in Rotated Array
  • Find Peak Element
  • Search in 2D Matrix

steps.title: Sorting Algorithms
steps.desc: Learn bubble, selection, insertion, merge, and quick sort with their complexities.
steps.theory:
Sorting Fundamentals

Sorting arranges elements in a specific order. Understanding sorting algorithms is crucial for optimizing other algorithms.

Comparison Sorts

  • Bubble Sort - O(n²) - Simple but inefficient
  • Selection Sort - O(n²) - Finds minimum element each pass
  • Insertion Sort - O(n²) - Efficient for small/partially sorted data
  • Merge Sort - O(n log n) - Divide and conquer, stable
  • Quick Sort - O(n log n) average - Fastest in practice

steps.title: Linked Lists
steps.desc: Master singly, doubly, and circular linked lists with pointer manipulation.
steps.theory:
Linked Lists

Linked lists store elements in nodes connected by pointers. Unlike arrays, they allow O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions.

Types

  • Singly Linked List - Each node points to next
  • Doubly Linked List - Each node points to both next and previous
  • Circular Linked List - Last node points back to first

Key Patterns

  • Fast & Slow Pointers (Floyd's Cycle Detection)
  • Dummy Node Technique
  • Reverse In-Place
  • Merge Two Sorted Lists

steps.title: Stacks & Queues
steps.desc: Understand LIFO and FIFO data structures and their practical applications.
steps.theory:
Stacks

Stack follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. Used in function calls, undo operations, and parsing.

Queues

Queue follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. Used in BFS, scheduling, and buffering.

Applications

  • Valid Parentheses (Stack)
  • Browser History (Stack)
  • Task Scheduling (Queue)
  • BFS Traversal (Queue)

steps.title: Hash Tables
steps.desc: Learn hash maps, sets, collision resolution, and their O(1) average operations.
steps.theory:
Hash Tables

Hash tables provide O(1) average-time lookups by mapping keys to values using a hash function. Essential for optimizing search-heavy algorithms.

Key Concepts

  • Hash Functions and Collisions
  • Chaining vs Open Addressing
  • Load Factor and Rehashing
  • Hash Set vs Hash Map

Applications

  • Two Sum - O(n) solution
  • Anagram Detection
  • Frequency Counting
  • Cache Implementation

quiz.question: What is the average time complexity of a hash table lookup?
quiz.explanation: With a good hash function and low load factor, hash tables provide O(1) average-time lookups.

quiz.question: What technique resolves collisions by storing multiple items in the same bucket?
quiz.explanation: Chaining stores all colliding items in a linked list at the same bucket index.

quiz.question: What happens when a hash table's load factor exceeds a threshold?
options: The table is rehashed with a larger size
quiz.explanation: Rehashing creates a larger table and re-inserts all entries to maintain O(1) performance.

steps.title: Trees & Binary Search Trees
steps.desc: Master tree traversals, BST operations, and recursive tree algorithms.
steps.theory:
Trees

Trees are hierarchical data structures. Binary trees have at most 2 children per node.

Traversals

  • Inorder (Left → Root → Right) - Sorted order for BST
  • Preorder (Root → Left → Right) - Tree copying
  • Postorder (Left → Right → Root) - Tree deletion
  • Level Order (BFS) - Level-by-level processing

Binary Search Tree (BST)

BST maintains the invariant: left subtree ≤ node < right subtree. Operations are O(log n) in balanced trees.

steps.title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
steps.desc: Learn DP fundamentals: memoization, tabulation, and solving classic problems.
steps.theory:
Dynamic Programming

DP = Recursion + Memoization. Solve complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems.

Key Properties

  • Optimal Substructure - Optimal solution uses optimal subsolutions
  • Overlapping Subproblems - Same subproblems solved multiple times

Approaches

  • Top-Down (Memoization) - Recursive with caching
  • Bottom-Up (Tabulation) - Iterative table filling

quiz.question: What are the two essential properties for a problem to be solvable with DP?
quiz.explanation: DP requires optimal substructure (best solution comes from best subsolutions) and overlapping subproblems (same subproblems recur).

quiz.question: Which approach caches results of recursive calls?
quiz.explanation: Memoization stores results of expensive function calls to avoid recomputation.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of DP Fibonacci?
quiz.explanation: DP Fibonacci runs in O(n) time by storing previously computed values instead of recalculating.

intermediate.title: Intermediate DSA Roadmap
intermediate.description: Master intermediate Data Structures & Algorithms concepts and build the foundation required for coding interviews at top tech companies.

steps.title: Advanced Arrays & Hashing
steps.desc: Master complex array manipulations, sliding window, two-pointer techniques, and advanced hashing patterns.
steps.theory:
Advanced Array Patterns

Go beyond basic arrays. Master complex patterns that appear frequently in interviews.

Patterns

  • Sliding Window - Track contiguous subarrays efficiently
  • Two Pointers - Trapping rain water, 3Sum, container with most water
  • Prefix Sum + HashMap - Subarray sum problems
  • Cyclic Sort - Missing/duplicate numbers

steps.title: Tree Algorithms & Traversals
steps.desc: Master advanced tree algorithms including LCA, diameter, serialization, and Morris traversal.
steps.theory:
Advanced Tree Algorithms

Go beyond basic tree traversals. Master advanced tree problems that appear in FAANG interviews.

Key Problems

  • Lowest Common Ancestor - Find the deepest common ancestor of two nodes
  • Tree Diameter - Longest path between any two nodes
  • Serialize/Deserialize - Convert tree to string and back
  • Morris Traversal - O(1) space traversal

steps.title: Graph Algorithms
steps.desc: Master BFS, DFS, topological sort, connected components, and cycle detection.
steps.theory:
Graph Fundamentals

Graphs model relationships. Learn essential traversal algorithms and their applications.

Key Algorithms

  • BFS - Shortest path, word ladder, level order traversal
  • DFS - Islands, cycle detection, topological sort
  • Union Find - Disjoint set operations, O(α(n))
  • Topological Sort - Course schedule, task ordering

steps.title: Dynamic Programming I
steps.desc: Master 1D DP, 2D DP, and classic interview problems like knapsack and LCS.
steps.theory:
Dynamic Programming Deep Dive

Systematic approach to solving DP problems.

5-Step Approach

  1. Define the state (what does dp[i] mean?)
  2. Write the recurrence relation
  3. Identify base cases
  4. Determine computation order
  5. Space optimization

Classic Problems

  • 0/1 Knapsack
  • Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
  • Coin Change
  • Edit Distance
  • Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)

steps.title: Heaps & Priority Queues
steps.desc: Master heap operations, heap sort, and problems like merge K sorted and top K frequent.
steps.theory:
Heaps

A heap is a complete binary tree where each parent is ≤ (min-heap) or ≥ (max-heap) its children.

Key Operations

  • Insert - Add element and bubble up
  • Extract Min/Max - Remove root and bubble down
  • Heapify - Build heap from array in O(n)

Applications

  • Priority Queue
  • Merge K Sorted Lists
  • Top K Frequent Elements
  • Find Median from Data Stream

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of inserting into a heap?
quiz.explanation: Insertion adds at the bottom and bubbles up, taking O(log n) time.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an array?
quiz.explanation: Heapify operation builds a heap in O(n) time using Floyd's algorithm.

quiz.question: Which data structure can find the median from a streaming data efficiently?
quiz.explanation: Use a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half to find median in O(1).

steps.title: Backtracking
steps.desc: Master recursive exploration, pruning, and classic problems like N-Queens and permutations.
steps.theory:
Backtracking

Backtracking explores all possible solutions by building candidates incrementally and abandoning them (pruning) when they cannot satisfy constraints.

Template

  1. Choose: Make a decision
  2. Explore: Recurse with the new state
  3. Unchoose: Undo the decision

Classic Problems

  • N-Queens
  • Permutations
  • Subsets
  • Combination Sum
  • Sudoku Solver

steps.title: Greedy Algorithms
steps.desc: Learn greedy choice property, exchange arguments, and classic greedy problems.
steps.theory:
Greedy Algorithms

Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find the global optimum.

When to use Greedy

  • Greedy Choice Property - Local optimum leads to global optimum
  • Optimal Substructure - Optimal solution contains optimal subsolutions

Classic Problems

  • Activity Selection
  • Huffman Coding
  • Coin Change (certain denominations)
  • Job Scheduling
  • Minimum Spanning Tree (Prim's & Kruskal's)

quiz.question: Which property must a problem have for a greedy algorithm to work?
quiz.explanation: The greedy choice property means that a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution.

quiz.question: Which algorithm finds the Minimum Spanning Tree?
quiz.explanation: Kruskal's (and Prim's) algorithms find the MST using a greedy approach.

steps.title: Interview Preparation & Review
steps.desc: Consolidate learning, practice mock interviews, and solve mixed-problem sets.
steps.theory:
Final Preparation

Consolidate all the topics learned. Practice solving mixed problems under time constraints.

Strategies

  • Review all patterns systematically
  • Practice on a whiteboard/online editor
  • Time yourself - solve problems in 30-45 minutes
  • Explain your thought process out loud
  • Prepare questions for interviewers

Key Areas to Focus

  • Data Structures: Arrays, Hash Tables, Trees, Graphs, Heaps
  • Algorithms: BFS/DFS, DP, Binary Search, Backtracking
  • System Design Basics (for senior roles)

quiz.question: Which approach is most effective for coding interview preparation?
quiz.explanation: Focus on consistent practice and recognizing patterns across different problems.

quiz.question: What should you do first when given a coding problem?
options: Understand the problem and ask clarifying questions
quiz.explanation: Always understand the problem thoroughly, ask clarifying questions, and discuss approaches before coding.

advanced.title: Advanced DSA Roadmap
advanced.description: Take your DSA skills to the next level! This roadmap covers advanced concepts required for FAANG interviews, competitive programming, and complex system design challenges.

steps.title: Advanced Arrays & Optimization
steps.desc: Master complex array manipulations, sliding window, and two-pointer techniques.
steps.theory:
Advanced Array Optimization: Optimizing array operations from O(N²) to O(N) or O(N log N).

Sliding Window: Used to track contiguous subarrays efficiently.

Trapping Rain Water Pattern: Two-pointer technique to solve complex optimization problems.

steps.title: Advanced Dynamic Programming
steps.desc: Learn advanced DP optimizations, multi-dimensional DP, and sequence matching techniques.
steps.theory:
Advanced DP Concepts: Identifying states with multiple dimensions.

Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS): Can be optimized from O(N²) to O(N log N).

Space Optimization: Reduce space complexity from O(N) to O(1) when state depends only on previous states.

steps.title: Advanced Graph Algorithms
steps.desc: Solve complex graph problems using shortest path, cycle detection, topological sorting, and BFS/DFS.
steps.theory:
Advanced Graphs: Complex graph traversal strategies.

Topological Sort: Ordering of vertices in a DAG.

Word Ladder (BFS State Space Search): BFS to find shortest path.

Dijkstra & Bellman-Ford: Shortest path algorithms for weighted graphs.

steps.title: Advanced Trees & Tries
steps.desc: Master advanced tree data structures, tries, segment trees, and Fenwick trees.
steps.theory:
Tries (Prefix Trees): Efficient for string search and autocomplete.

Segment Trees: Range queries and updates in O(log n).

Fenwick Tree (BIT): Efficient prefix sum queries and updates.

Red-Black & AVL Trees: Self-balancing BST for guaranteed O(log n) operations.

steps.title: String Algorithms
steps.desc: Master advanced string algorithms: KMP, Rabin-Karp, Z-algorithm, and suffix arrays.
steps.theory:
Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP): O(n+m) pattern matching using prefix function.

Rabin-Karp: Rolling hash for efficient substring matching.

Z-Algorithm: Linear time pattern matching using Z-array.

Suffix Arrays: Powerful for string processing problems.

steps.title: Advanced Graph Algorithms II
steps.desc: Master advanced graph algorithms: strongly connected components, max flow, and MST.
steps.theory:
Strongly Connected Components (SCC): Kosaraju's and Tarjan's algorithms.

Maximum Flow: Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp, Dinic's algorithm.

Minimum Cut: Applications of max-flow min-cut theorem.

MST Variations: Prim's with heap optimization, Kruskal's with Union-Find.

quiz.question: Which algorithm finds Strongly Connected Components using two passes?
quiz.explanation: Kosaraju's algorithm uses two DFS passes to find SCCs.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of Edmonds-Karp max flow algorithm?
quiz.explanation: Edmonds-Karp runs in O(V * E²) time using BFS for augmenting paths.

quiz.question: What data structure optimizes Prim's algorithm for dense graphs?
quiz.explanation: Fibonacci heap gives O(E + V log V) - best for dense graphs.

steps.title: System Design Basics
steps.desc: Learn system design fundamentals: scalability, load balancing, caching, and databases.
steps.theory:
System Design Fundamentals: Essential concepts for senior-level interviews.

Key Concepts: Vertical vs horizontal scaling, load balancing, caching strategies, CDN, database sharding, CAP theorem, consistent hashing.

Design Problems: Design URL shortener, design Twitter, design Uber, design chat system.

quiz.question: Which theorem states that a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance?
quiz.explanation: CAP theorem (also known as Brewer's theorem) states you can only guarantee 2 out of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.

quiz.question: Which caching strategy evicts the least recently used items first?
quiz.explanation: LRU (Least Recently Used) evicts items that haven't been accessed for the longest time.

steps.title: Bit Manipulation & Math
steps.desc: Master bitwise operations, bit tricks, and competitive math techniques.
steps.theory:
Bit Manipulation: Efficient operations using bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>).

Common Tricks: Check if power of 2, count set bits, find unique element, XOR tricks.

Mathematical Algorithms: Sieve of Eratosthenes, GCD/LCM, modular arithmetic, fast exponentiation.

steps.title: Competitive Programming Techniques
steps.desc: Master advanced problem-solving techniques for competitive programming contests.
steps.theory:
Competitive Programming: Techniques optimized for speed and efficiency under contest constraints.

Key Techniques: Meet in the middle, Mo's algorithm, square root decomposition, binary indexed trees, segment trees with lazy propagation, heavy-light decomposition.

Contest Strategy: Problem categorization, time management, debugging under pressure.

quiz.question: Which technique splits the search space into two halves and combines results?
quiz.explanation: Meet in the middle splits the problem into two halves, solves each, and combines results. Often reduces O(2^n) to O(2^(n/2)).

quiz.question: What is Mo's algorithm used for?
quiz.explanation: Mo's algorithm answers range queries offline by reordering them for efficient processing.

steps.title: Advanced Optimization & Interview Strategies
steps.desc: Master interview-level optimization techniques, concurrency, and advanced problem-solving strategies.
steps.theory:
Final Interview Strategies: Optimal time/space balances.

Multithreading & Concurrency: Understanding parallel algorithms, race conditions, deadlocks.

Memory Optimization: Cache-friendly algorithms, memory pools, object pooling.

Backtracking Pruning: Cutting off recursive paths early with branch and bound.

quiz.question: Which technique is most appropriate for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
quiz.explanation: BFS explores layer by layer and is guaranteed to find the shortest path.

quiz.question: What is the optimal time complexity of LIS?
quiz.explanation: LIS can be solved in O(N log N) using DP with binary search.

quiz.question: How can we optimize space complexity of House Robber from O(N) to O(1)?
options: Keeping track of last two values
quiz.explanation: Since each state only depends on the previous two states, we only need two variables.
